title: Chapter 49: The Sit-In at Home
content: Saturday, and Chloe was in Boston, so the sit-in happened without her.

Which was the point. That was the whole point. The community was the practice, not the capybara. Jordan kept saying it out loud, partly to remind everyone else and partly because she still needed to hear it herself.

Prospect Park, Long Meadow, the same stretch of grass they'd been using for eleven days. Twenty-nine people showed up this time — a few fewer than when Chloe was there, but a few were new, people who'd heard about it from other people who'd heard about it from someone's Instagram story that didn't tag anyone or explain anything, just a video of thirty humans sitting in a circle on blankets with their eyes closed, and the caption said *come if you want*.
So they came.

Dave spread his blanket next to Jordan's. Mrs. Hendricks had the folding chair she always brought, positioned at the edge of the circle like she was keeping watch. Maya set up near the center, cross-legged, hands on her knees, already breathing.
And then Amara arrived.

She hadn't been invited. Maya hadn't asked her to come. Amara had followed her out of the apartment — Maya had noticed halfway down the subway stairs, turned around, and found her mother standing on the sidewalk in Saturday casual, which for Amara meant tailored linen trousers and a silk-cotton blend top that probably cost more than Maya's rent.
"What are you doing?" Maya had asked.
"I'm coming with you."
"You don't—"
"I'm coming with you, Mayadevi."
Not *Maya*. *Mayadevi*. The full name. The name that meant *this is not a discussion*. Maya had debated pressing it, read her mother's expression, and decided this was not the hill.
So Amara came.

She stood at the edge of the circle for a long moment, assessing the layout with the same expression she used to evaluate merger proposals. Blankets. People. Grass. No structure. No leader. No presentation. No agenda.
Jordan tilted her head toward the open spot next to Mrs. Hendricks's folding chair. "There's room."
Amara looked at the spot. Looked at Mrs. Hendricks. Looked at the spot again.
"Thank you," she said, and walked over.

Mrs. Hendricks glanced up as Amara settled onto the grass beside her chair. Amara was very visibly not comfortable on the ground — she shifted, adjusted, tried three different leg configurations before settling on one that was, by her standards, merely uncomfortable rather than intolerable.

Mrs. Hendricks didn't say anything. She reached into her bag and pulled out a tangerine. She peeled it slowly, the way she did everything — deliberate, unhurried, like time was a thing that waited for her instead of the other way around. She separated a segment and held it out to Amara.
Amara looked at it.
A piece of fruit. Offered by a stranger. On the grass. In a park. On a Saturday. When she could be at home reading the Financial Times and drinking Darjeeling from the cup that had been her mother's.
She took it.
They didn't speak. They sat. Amara ate the tangerine segment. Mrs. Hendricks ate one too. The circle breathed.

It was softer without Chloe. That was the honest word. When Chloe was there, the hum arrived like a tide — it pulled at everyone, deepened the breath, opened something behind the sternum. Without her, the breathing was just breathing. The sitting was just sitting.
But it worked.

The community breathed together, and something happened. Not the tide. More like a creek. A quiet, continuous flow of attention that pooled and spread and made the grass feel different, made the air feel different, made twenty-nine people sitting on blankets in a park feel like something other than twenty-nine separate people sitting on blankets in a park.
Dave felt it. Softer, yes. But real.

Twenty minutes in, he checked his phone under the blanket, which was technically against the vibe but whatever, he was worried. Chloe hadn't texted since Friday. He'd sent her three messages that she'd left on read, which — fine, she was busy, she was being studied, she had a whole thing. But he worried.
No new messages.
He put the phone away and breathed.

At the thirty-minute mark, Amara shifted beside Mrs. Hendricks. She'd been sitting with her hands on her knees, eyes closed, for the entire half hour. Her phone sat untouched on the grass beside her. Dave caught Maya glancing over at her mom, doing a visible double-take at the phone's unattended state.
Thirty-two minutes. Thirty-three.
At thirty-five, Amara opened her eyes.
Mrs. Hendricks offered another tangerine segment. Amara took it again.
"That was..." Amara paused, searching for a word that could survive her standards. "...pleasant."
Maya, from across the circle, without opening her eyes: "High praise."
"Don't be smart."
But there was the smallest smile on Amara's face. The kind of smile that had probably been arraigned in front of a committee before being granted approval for external display.
Mrs. Hendricks saw it. She didn't comment. She just offered a third segment.
Amara took it.
Dave's phone buzzed.
He checked it under the blanket again. A text from Chloe. No words — just a photo.
Her room at Meridian. He tilted the screen so the brightness wouldn't disturb anyone and looked at it carefully.

The room was nice. Genuinely nice. Clean lines, warm lighting, what looked like hardwood floors with underfloor heating, because of course Meridian had heated floors. A bed with a nice comforter. A window. A small desk. A plant in the corner — probably fake, but a tasteful fake.
Everything a person could need.

He stared at it, and the longer he looked, the more he saw what Chloe was showing him. The room was a habitat. Temperature-controlled, monitored, aesthetically pleasant. Designed for comfort. Designed for observation.
She was the exhibit.

The plant was the worst part. The detail that cracked it open. A decorative plant in a research facility room where someone was being studied. Like a water feature in a zoo enclosure. *We want you to be comfortable. We want you to behave naturally.*
He locked his phone and put it face-down on the blanket.
The sit-in ended at forty-five minutes. People packed up slowly, talking in low voices, the way people talked after meditating — like the air was still moving and they didn't want to disturb it.
Amara stood, brushing grass off her linen trousers with the focused displeasure of someone who had just discovered grass existed. Mrs. Hendricks folded her chair.
"Same time next week?" Mrs. Hendricks asked.
Amara considered this. "I'll check my schedule."

Mrs. Hendricks nodded as if this were a perfectly normal response to an invitation to sit in a park. She handed Amara the remaining half of the tangerine. Amara looked at it, then at Mrs. Hendricks, and took it.
Maya watched her mother walk toward the subway entrance, tangerine in hand, and something in her chest did a thing she didn't have a word for.

Dave walked home alone, Chloe's photo still burning a hole in his phone. He thought about habitats. He thought about exhibits. He thought about the way a room could be beautiful and a cage at the same time and how the cage was always nicer when someone wanted you to stay in it.
He didn't text her back. He didn't know what to say.
*That room looks like a habitat and you're the exhibit* felt too true to send.
*I miss you* felt too small.
*Come home* wasn't his call.
So he walked, and the hum stayed soft in his chest — not the river it was with Chloe, but a creek, a trickle, enough. Enough to know it was still there. Enough to know it was real.
The practice existed without her.
It just existed better with her.
